posts.title: Predicting Global Life Expectancy Using Public Health and Socioeconomic Indicators
posts.authors: Naomi Alvarado, Jae Rothrock, Cameron Hajaliloo
posts.keywords: Statistical Modeling, Public Health Analytics, Predictive Modeling, Life Expectancy, Global Health, Health Economics, Data-Driven Policy, Applied Statistics, Population Health, International Development, Humanitarian Analytics, Regression Analysis, Public Health Surveillance, Health Equity, Quantitative Research
posts.abstract: The World Health Organization (WHO) found that average life expectancy has increased by over 6 years in the last two decades. However, this begs the question: how do we continue this trend? This project explores whether external indicators can consistently predict a country’s average life expectancy. Using a subset of indicators from the WHO’s World Health Annual Statistics Report, we built a model to predict life expectancy across countries in 2002. First, we conducted model selection to identify the best subset of predictors for life expectancy. Then, we conducted model adequacy checks to ensure all model assumptions were met. Furthermore, we extensively analyzed potential influence points and their impact on our model. Our final model included the percentage of expenditure, average adult BMI, the number of reported HIV/AIDS cases, the number of reported polio cases, and income resource composition as predictors of life expectancy, which we further contextualized using external sources. This model explained 84% of the variability in life expectancy, leaving 16% unexplained. Additional metrics were used to assess the bias and complexity of our model. A significant takeaway from our findings is that traceable indicators, many of which are systematically reported to the World Health Organization, are statistically significant predictors of life expectancy outcomes. From both regulatory and economic perspectives, these results highlight the value of robust public health surveillance and data-driven intervention strategies in informing policy allocation, emergency response planning, and long-term development initiatives. More broadly, the findings underscore the humanitarian importance of leveraging measurable population health indicators to support earlier, more targeted efforts to reduce disparities, improve quality of life, and protect vulnerable populations on a global scale.
posts.status: Undergraduate Research Project, Independent Research Conference Presentation, Working Paper Manuscript in Preparation, Presented at the Seventeenth Annual Loyola Marymount University Undergraduate Research Symposium
